title: How to Pile the Pain on Putin
content:
Energy security matters. Ukraine’s recent campaign against Russia’s oil infrastructure marks a visible and strategic shift in precisely how it is applying pressure on the Russian war economy. Through well-judged measures, the West can now join in.

Over the past few months, Ukrainian forces have repeatedly struck oil refineries, fuel depots, and storage facilities across Russia. More recently, Ukraine expanded this pressure to maritime logistics: since the beginning of July, Ukraine has substantially increased drone attacks against vessels linked to Russia’s shadow fleet in the Sea of Azov, disrupting fuel deliveries to occupied Crimea and forcing Russia to temporarily halt shipping through the Don-Azov Channel. Ukraine claimed to have struck 136 vessels in the 10 days to July 15. These operations not only disrupt Russia’s ability to process oil but also its ability to transport and monetize it. Wheat shipments have also been hit.
snippet: Energy security matters. Ukraine’s recent campaign against Russia’s oil infrastructure marks a visible and strategic shift in precisely how it is applying pressure on the Russian war economy. Through
creator: Andrew Raynus

title: European Court Battles: Tech Loses an Ally
content:
The verdicts are damning. 

Europe’s top court recently rejected Apple’s challenge to EU rules that designate its app stores and iOS operating system “gatekeepers.” It upheld the European Commission’s record €4.1 billion antitrust fine against Google for abusing its Android market dominance, and backed a €2.4 billion penalty against the search company for unfairly favoring its own comparison shopping services.

The significant losses send an important signal: Silicon Valley should not count on Luxembourg-based judges to dilute European antitrust enforcement. Historically, judges required exhaustive, case-by-case proof that specific behavior directly harmed consumers. They accepted tech companies’ defenses that consumers preferred bundled services or lock-ins, and overturned significant regulatory fines.
creator: Bill Echikson

title: Russia’s Raddled Stock Market
content:
Russia’s stock market is stuck in one of its worst stretches in decades. The MOEX index has now fallen for 17 straight weeks — the longest unbroken decline since 1997 — and is back down near the lows it hit on the day Russia invaded Ukraine in February 2022.

Four and a half years of war have brought the market full circle: a catastrophic crash, a partial recovery, and now a slide that has erased most of the gains in between. Investors who bought in at the start of the war have made almost nothing since.

The immediate trigger was a Central Bank decision last month to cut interest rates by less than markets expected, a signal that officials are worried about inflation, high state spending, and the risk of renewed sanctions on oil. High base rates ensure that deposit rates at the banks are so high that no stock market investment can compete with them, prompting even more stock sales.
snippet: Russia’s stock market is stuck in one of its worst stretches in decades. The MOEX index has now fallen for 17 straight weeks — the longest unbroken decline since 1997 — and is back down near the lows
creator: Andrew Raynus

title: Ukrainian Military Reform: Better But Not Perfect
content:
The appointment of Mykhailo Fedorov as minister of defense in January followed years of criticism of corruption and inefficiency, and complaints that the ministry was detached from the needs of active personnel, veterans, and new recruits.

Known for his focus on digital governance, fast implementation, and data-driven management, Fedorov promised a deep audit of the Defense Ministry and the Armed Forces. The plan was not only to find money and fight corruption, but to make mobilization sustainable and national service fair. On July 15, he was sacked for what he claimed was a campaign by the old guard around Commander-in-Chief Syrskyi.

There were mass protests in Kyiv following his dismissal. President Zelenskyy said he could not allow the feuding between Fedorov and Syrskyi to continue.

His six months in charge won over many of the public, 50% of whom said they trusted him in a June poll, and among foreign diplomats.
snippet: The appointment of Mykhailo Fedorov as minister of defense in January followed years of criticism of corruption and inefficiency, and complaints that the ministry was detached from the needs of active
creator: Andrew Raynus

title: The Kremlin Smiles on Russia’s Rock’n’Roll Renaissance
content:
In the late 1980s, Viktor Tsoi and KINO captured and advanced the spirit of perestroika and the hope of a new Russia emerging from the wreckage of the USSR. 

While it was not an overtly political band, as the Russian music critic Artemy Troitskii contends, KINO’s lyrics, music, and style reflected individualism, freedom from state control, and shared globalism. 

And the band was certainly anti-war, with many songs about the soldier experience in Afghanistan in their stadium-filling repertoire. 

Many have asked what Tsoi, who died in 1990, would have said about Russia’s War on Ukraine. Other famous musicians of that era, including Boris Grebenshikov, Yuri Shevchuk, and Andrey Makarevich, as well as Alla Pugacheva, have all left Russia and spoken out against Vladimir Putin and his “special military operation.”  

Given KINO’s enduring popularity and its songs demanding change and decrying the insanity and brutality of war, one could imagine the Kremlin wanting it to fade away too.

But its music not only lives on; the state is actively promoting it.
snippet: In the late 1980s, Viktor Tsoi and KINO captured and advanced the spirit of perestroika and the hope of a new Russia emerging from the wreckage of the USSR.
creator: Andrew Raynus

title: The Black Sea Needs a NATO Sentry
content:
The European Union (EU) has approved one of its most strategically important energy infrastructure projects: the Black Sea Connectivity Submarine Electricity Cable. Yet while Brussels is offering €2.3bn ($2.6bn) for the program, neither the EU nor NATO has developed a security system capable of protecting it from the type of shadow war attacks Russia has launched elsewhere. The alliance needs to act, and swiftly. 

The growing frequency of suspected sabotage incidents, from the Baltic and Black seas to the South China Sea, underscores that resilience in contested waters is no longer merely a critical infrastructure challenge but a strategic imperative. Diversifying energy sources and supply chains remains the most effective long-term strategy for Brussels to reduce dependence on Russia and limit its capacity for coercion. But diversification means little if the new infrastructure cannot be defended, which is precisely the test the Black Sea Submarine Cable now poses. 

The bloc does recognize the project’s importance. This year, it designated the cable a Project of Mutual Interest (PMI), a status that unlocks access to EU funding and streamlined regulatory procedures.
snippet: The European Union (EU) has approved one of its most strategically important energy infrastructure projects: the Black Sea Connectivity Submarine Electricity Cable. Yet while Brussels is offering
creator: Jim Lewis

title: The Moldova-Romania Unification Riddle
content:
The once far-fetched idea is being discussed not only in Chișinău and Bucharest, but also in Brussels, which says the two nations should decide their own fates. For Moscow, it represents a nightmare. 

Moldova was part of Romania from 1918 until 1940, when it was annexed by the Soviet Union as part of Moscow’s pact with Nazi Germany. It declared independence when the USSR collapsed in 1991.

A nation of 2.3 million people, Moldova lies between Ukraine and Romania which is a member of both the European Union and NATO. Unification would require referendums in both countries and is complicated by frozen conflicts in the separatist republic of Transnistria and the autonomous region of Gagauzia, both of which have strong ties to Russia and oppose unification.

“Unification is being discussed now because of the [Ukraine] war and geopolitical realignment,” said Armand Gosu, associate professor at Bucharest University’s Faculty of Political Sciences. “The chances are low, but they do exist.”
snippet: The once far-fetched idea is being discussed not only in Chișinău and Bucharest, but also in Brussels, which says the two nations should decide their own fates. For Moscow, it represents a nightmare.
creator: Andrew Raynus

title: Ukraine Strikes Russia’s Central Nervous System
content:
For most of the full-scale war, Russia enjoyed a dangerous strategic asymmetry. It could destroy Ukraine’s power plants, refineries, heating systems, and civilian infrastructure, while much of Russia’s own war economy remained far behind the front line and largely out of Ukraine’s reach.

Not anymore.

Ukraine appears to have found one of the few pressure points capable of changing the Kremlin’s cost-benefit calculation: the oil industry. The purpose is not symbolic retaliation. It is to raise the economic, military, and political cost of continuing the war until Moscow understands that prolonging it may become more expensive than ending it.

This logic was clear from the beginning of the invasion. Ukraine had to convince Russia that the war would become unaffordable. Yet in 2022 and 2023, it lacked the long-range weapons to systematically strike the infrastructure that funds the Kremlin’s war machine.
snippet: For most of the full-scale war, Russia enjoyed a dangerous strategic asymmetry. It could destroy Ukraine’s power plants, refineries, heating systems, and civilian infrastructure, while much of
creator: Polina Tsurikova

title: Ireland’s Digital Tightrope: Balancing EU Sovereignty Against US Tech Wrath
content:
Ireland took over the rotating chair of Council, which houses member states’ governments, and with it digital policymaking at a moment when the bloc is debating a new Tech Sovereignty package that favors European suppliers and raises barriers to foreign imports. The proposed European regulations have set off alarm bells in Washington. 

Few countries have more at stake. The US is Ireland’s biggest foreign investor, accounting for €800 billion, according to the Irish Central Statistics Office. The country is home to the European headquarters of many of America’s biggest companies, from tech stalwarts Alphabet and Apple to pharma giants Eli Lilly and Pfizer.

Ireland aims to avoid taking sides in digital disputes, determined to preserve a workable transatlantic tech framework. Between July and December, the country hopes to forge a European consensus negotiating position on the Chips Act 2.0, steer the contentious Digital Omnibus, and manage a Cloud and AI Development Act whose sovereignty clauses worry major American cloud providers operating in Europe.
snippet: Ireland took over the rotating chair of Council, which houses member states’ governments, and with it digital policymaking at a moment when the bloc is debating a new Tech Sovereignty package that
creator: Lucinda Creighton

title: Russia’s Spies: Smear Recycling
content:
It is a time of huge strain on the Russian despotism. Drones strike oil refineries with near-clockwork regularity, fuel queues stretch for miles from filling stations, and the leader’s aura of invincibility is under question. So it might seem a curious time for Russia’s guard dogs to be dredging up old conspiracy theories about Osama bin Laden.

Or perhaps not. The security services, always prone to explaining difficulties and failures with reference to the evil intent of the West, are returning to the favored stories of old. 

So it was that Nikolay Patrushev, one of Putin’s closest allies and a former FSB chief, claimed that Osama bin Laden, the infamous al-Qaeda leader, was a CIA asset until he began to operate free from its guiding hand.
snippet: It is a time of huge strain on the Russian despotism. Drones strike oil refineries with near-clockwork regularity, fuel queues stretch for miles from filling stations, and the leader’s aura of
creator: Irina Borogan
